# Using The Control Group Mean


Now that we have spoken a little about averaging as an idea, we can finally formalize this in a panel data setting such that it is useful. Before, I was sort of vague about using averages for comparison; I gave a plot, but I never said how we would more formally do this. This chapter explains how we would do this as a first pass.

## Two Way Fixed Effects

Recall the [linear factor model](poframe.html#assump-lfm) as a data generating process. The original model assumes that some number of factor loadings and common time factors interact (along with covariates, potentially) to generate outcomes. However, this can be simplified by a lot to what we traditionally know as the two-way fixed effects model. 

::: {#assump-twfe .assumption title="Two-Way Fixed Effects Model" data-link="did.html#assump-twfe"}
$\forall j \in \mathcal{N}, : t \in \mathcal{T}_{1}$, pre-treatment outcomes are generated according to

$$
y_{jt}(0) = \mu_j + \lambda_t + \varepsilon_{jt},
$$

where $\mu_j$ are *unit-specific intercepts* capturing time-invariant heterogeneity, $\lambda_t$ are *time-specific effects* capturing shocks or trends common to all units, and $\varepsilon_{jt}$ are zero-mean *idiosyncratic errors*. This model is a special case of the [linear factor model](poframe.html#assump-lfm), where there is a single factor with identical loadings across all units:
$$
\boldsymbol{\lambda}_t = [1, \lambda_t]^\top, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_j = [\mu_j, 1]^\top.
$$
In this representation, the first component captures unit-level heterogeneity, while the second captures the shared temporal component.
:::

Intuitively, the TWFE model assumes that all units respond *equally* to the same common time trend $\lambda_t$, differing only in their fixed intercepts $\mu_j$. This simplicity makes it powerful for many policy applications but also restrictive — if units react differently to aggregate shocks (i.e. have heterogeneous loadings on $\lambda_t$), the model is misspecified, and DID-type estimators may become biased. In that sense, the LFM generalizes TWFE by allowing these sensitivities to vary across units. @fig-dag-twfe represents this DGP graphically.


```{python}

#| echo: False
#| label: fig-dag-twfe
#| fig-cap: "DAG for TWFE Factor Model"


import networkx as nx
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scmbook.style import set_book_style
set_book_style()

# Create DAG
G = nx.DiGraph()

# Nodes
nodes = [
    r'$\mu_j$',           # unit factor
    r'$\lambda_t$',       # time factor
    r'$y_{jt}$',          # observed outcome
    r'$\bar{y}_{0t}$'     # avg controls (estimate lambda_t)
]
G.add_nodes_from(nodes)

# Edges
edges = [
    (r'$\mu_j$', r'$y_{jt}$'),
    (r'$\lambda_t$', r'$y_{jt}$'),
    (r'$y_{jt}$', r'$\bar{y}_{0t}$')
]
G.add_edges_from(edges)

# Draw the DAG with nx.draw_planar and styling
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
nx.draw_planar(
    G,
    with_labels=True,
    node_size=2500,
    node_color=["#add8e6", "#add8e6", "#ff7f7f", "#90ee90"],  # blue, blue, red, green
    width=2,
    font_size=14,
    arrowsize=20
)
plt.title("Factor Model DAG (TWFE / Additive Factor Model)")
plt.show()




```

To make this concrete, consider a toy example with two units over two time periods. Consider two units ($j = 1, 2$) observed over two time periods ($t = 1, 2$). Suppose

$$
y_{jt} = \mu_j + \lambda_t + \varepsilon_{jt}
$$

holds. Let the fixed effects be

$$
\mu =
\begin{bmatrix}
2 \\
5
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
\lambda =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \\
3
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

Ignoring the random errors $\varepsilon_{jt}$, we can represent the predicted outcomes as a matrix where each row corresponds to a time period and each column to a unit:

$$
M =
\begin{bmatrix}
y_{11} & y_{21} \\
y_{12} & y_{22}
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\mu_1 + \lambda_1 & \mu_2 + \lambda_1 \\
\mu_1 + \lambda_2 & \mu_2 + \lambda_2
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
2 + 1 & 5 + 1 \\
2 + 3 & 5 + 3
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
3 & 6 \\
5 & 8
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

In this example, moving from the first to the second row adds the same amount, $+2$, to each unit because $\lambda_t$ changes by the same amount for everyone. 


::: {.problem title="Additive Factor Model" data-link="#problem-additive-factor-model"}
Explain in your own words what the additive factor model 

$$
y_{jt} = \mu_j + \lambda_t + \varepsilon_{jt}
$$ 

represents. What do the terms $\mu_j$ and $\lambda_t$ capture in practical terms?
:::


::: {.problem title="Unit Effects vs. Time Effects" data-link="#problem-unit-vs-time"}
Give an example of a real-world scenario where two units might share $\lambda_t$ but differ in $\mu_j$. How would this difference affect naive comparisons of levels?
:::


## Demeaning and Difference-in-Differences

A simple way to approximate the counterfactual outcome for a treated unit is to use the mean of the control group. That is, we could take the average outcome of untreated units and use it as an estimate of what the treated unit would have experienced without treatment. However, using the mean alone implicitly assumes that all units start from the same level—that is, that their $\mu_j$ are identical. In practice, this assumption is rarely justified. Units may differ in income, size, resources, or other characteristics long before any intervention occurs. Ignoring these baseline differences can be misleading: post-treatment differences might simply reflect where units began, not the causal effect of the treatment. This is the basic intuition behind DID. To formalize this idea, we can difference out these time-invariant differences via *demeaning*.

::: {#lem-demean-trends .lemma title="Demeaning"}

## Demeaning

Let the observed outcome for unit $j$ at time $t$ in the pre-treatment periods $\mathcal{T}_1$ follow the additive two-way fixed effects model
$$
y_{jt} = \mu_j + \lambda_t + \varepsilon_{jt}, \qquad t \in \mathcal{T}_1,
$$
where $\mu_j$ is a unit-specific baseline, $\lambda_t$ a common time effect, and $\varepsilon_{jt}$ an idiosyncratic error with finite mean. Define the pre-period demeaned outcome
$$
r_{jt} \defeq y_{jt} - \frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}_1|}\sum_{s \in \mathcal{T}_1} y_{js}.
$$
Then for every $j$ and $t \in \mathcal{T}_1$,
$$
r_{jt} = (\lambda_t - \bar{\lambda}) + (\varepsilon_{jt} - \bar{\varepsilon}_j),
$$
where $\displaystyle \bar{\lambda} := \frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}_1|}\sum_{s \in \mathcal{T}_1}\lambda_s$ and $\displaystyle \bar{\varepsilon}_j := \frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}_1|}\sum_{s \in \mathcal{T}_1}\varepsilon_{js}$. In particular, the unit baseline $\mu_j$ cancels exactly, so $r_{jt}$ depends only on deviations of time effects and idiosyncratic errors from their pre-period averages.
:::

Demeaning is a fundamental idea in econometrics; see, for example, the review by @demscmrev. Rather than comparing treated and control units at a single moment based on current levels, we examine how their outcomes *evolve* across time. Specifically, we compare the *change* in the treated unit before and after treatment to the *change* observed in untreated units over the same period. By focusing on relative changes, we adjust for baseline differences.  The crucial insight is this: what matters is not that two units start at the same level, but that their *pre-treatment trends move similarly*. If trajectories are parallel before treatment, any divergence afterward can be attributed to the treatment itself. This shift from levels to trends is precisely what the Difference-in-Differences estimator formalizes.

::: {.problem title="Baseline Differences" data-link="#problem-baseline-differences"}
Why is it important to adjust for baseline differences between treated and control units when estimating treatment effects? How does DID address this?
:::



## Counterfactuals Under DID

Suppose we have a setting where a group of units is treated all at once, while others are never treated. The simplest form of the DID estimator can be obtained by minimizing the distance between the treated unit’s outcomes and the average of untreated units, up to an additive constant $\beta$ that adjusts for baseline differences. The counterfactual for the DID method is simply the mean of the control group plus some constant.

::: {#def-ddcounterfactual}

## DID Counterfactual

The post-treatment counterfactual for the treated unit under DID can be expressed as

$$
\hat{y}_{1t}(0) = \bar{y}_{0t} + \beta^\ast, \quad t > T_0,
$$

where

$$
\bar{y}_{0t} \coloneqq \frac{1}{N_0} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} y_{jt}.
$$

Here, $N_0 = |\mathcal{N}_0|$ is the number of untreated units, so that each control unit receives an equal weight of $N_0^{-1}$. $\beta$ is a scalar which is the mean difference between the treated and control group in the pre-treatment period.
:::

A natural extension of this is the idea of DID as a centering estimator. 


::: {#lem-centering}

## Centered DID



For any fixed donor pool $\mathcal{N}_0$, the DID estimator for a single treated unit has a closed-form solution

$$
\beta^\ast = \frac{1}{T_0} \sum_{t=1}^{T_0} \bigl(y_{1t} - \bar{y}_{\mathcal{N}_0t}\bigr),
\qquad
\bar{y}_{\mathcal{N}_0t} = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_0|} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} y_{jt}.
$$

The residual vector after accounting for this optimal intercept is

$$
\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{y}_c - \bar{\mathbf{y}}_{c,\mathcal{N}_0},
\qquad
\mathbf{y}_c = \mathbf{y}_1 - \bar{y}_1 \mathbf{1}_{T_0},
\qquad
\bar{\mathbf{y}}_{c,\mathcal{N}_0} = \bar{\mathbf{y}}_{\mathcal{N}_0} - \bar{m}_{\mathcal{N}_0} \mathbf{1}_{T_0}.
$$

Consequently, the pre-treatment sum of squared residuals is

$$
\ell_{\text{DID}}(\mathcal{N}_0) = \|\mathbf{y}_c - \bar{\mathbf{y}}_{c,\mathcal{N}_0}\|_2^2.
$$



:::

Even if the treated and control units have different levels, the demeaning removes these level differences. DID relies on the Parallel Trends Assumption (PTA), which states that, in the absence of treatment, the expected difference between the treated unit and the control group would remain constant over time — across *every* period, pre-treatment and post-treatment alike:

::: {#def-pta .definition title="Parallel Trends Assumption"}
In the absence of treatment,
$$
\mathbb{E}[y_{1t}(0) - \bar{y}_{0t}(0)] = \mathbb{E}[y_{1t'}(0) - \bar{y}_{0t'}(0)], \qquad \forall \, t, t' \in \mathcal{T}_1 \cup \mathcal{T}_2.
$$
:::

The range of the quantifier is the whole point. The equality must hold for all periods, including the post-treatment periods $\mathcal{T}_2$, where the treated unit's untreated outcome $y_{1t}(0)$ is precisely the counterfactual we are trying to recover. This is what makes DID identify a causal effect: it is the parallel movement of $y_{1t}(0)$ and $\bar{y}_{0t}(0)$ *after* treatment that lets us read post-treatment divergence as the treatment's doing. It is also why the assumption is fundamentally untestable — the post-treatment $y_{1t}(0)$ is never observed, so we can never check the very periods that do the identifying work.

It is worth being clear about how this relates to the additive [two-way fixed effects model](did.html#assump-twfe). If every unit shares the same time effect $\lambda_t$, their untreated outcomes move in lockstep and parallel trends holds automatically. But the additive model is stronger than DID actually needs: identification requires only that trends be parallel *on average*, in the sense of the expectation above, not that outcomes follow a literal additive structure with well-behaved idiosyncratic errors. Parallel trends is the primitive assumption; the two-way fixed effects model is merely a convenient sufficient condition for it.

Because the Parallel Trends Assumption cannot be checked, econometricians fall back on the pre-treatment analogue, which we *can* inspect:

::: {#def-ppta .definition title="Parallel Pre-Trends Assumption"}
$$
\mathbb{E}\Big[y_{1t}(0) - N_{0}^{-1} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} y_{jt}(0)\Big] = \beta, \quad \forall \, t \in \mathcal{T}_{1}.
$$
:::

The two assumptions differ only in their range: the Parallel Trends Assumption quantifies over all periods $\mathcal{T}_1 \cup \mathcal{T}_2$, whereas the Parallel Pre-Trends Assumption restricts attention to the pre-treatment periods $\mathcal{T}_1$. Parallel pre-trends is therefore a *testable implication* of parallel trends, not the identifying assumption itself — it is necessary but not sufficient. @Li2022augmented put the split cleanly: parallel trends divides into a testable part, that the assumption at least holds in the pretreatment period, and a nontestable part, that the same relationship between treated and control units carries into the posttreatment period, when the counterfactual is unobserved. Pre-treatment differences can look perfectly constant and yet the trends may still diverge after treatment for reasons that have nothing to do with the intervention. When the pre-trend condition holds, and assuming additionally no anticipation and SUTVA (see @DIDmodern for more), we generally treat the DID estimator as unbiased. But this remains a judgment about the unobserved post-period, extrapolated from the pre-period, rather than something the data can ever confirm.


::: {.problem title="Parallel Trends Assumption" data-link="#problem-parallel-trends"}
Define the parallel trends assumption formally. How can you assess whether this assumption is plausible in pre-treatment data? You may integrate ideas from the recent staggered DID literature, if you wish.
:::

::: {.problem title="Counterfactual Construction" data-link="#problem-counterfactual-construction"}
Explain how the DID estimator, as discussed above, constructs the counterfactual for a treated unit. How does using the mean of control units approximate the unobserved $\lambda_t$?
:::


## Parallel Trends in Small $N$ Settings

DID is often *applied* in settings with many units and few time periods [@DIDmodern], but the identification argument itself asks only for at least one treated and one control group observed before and after treatment — the "large $N$" setting is a common use case, not a requirement. In cases of small $N$, the parallel pre-trends assumption can be less likely to hold. To illustrate, consider the canonical paper on SCM by @Abadie2010, which studies the impact of California's Anti-Tobacco Campaign, Proposition 99, on cigarette sales per capita. Enacted in 1989, Prop 99 attempted to reduce smoking via a statewide public health campaign. In the original dataset, the authors had 38 control states to work with from 1970 to 2000. Using the estimator described above, we will use Python to estimate the DID design for the effect of Prop 99 on tobacco consumption. To follow along, you'll need the `dataprep` helper from my mlsynth code, which may be installed like


```{shell}
pip install -U git+https://github.com/jgreathouse9/mlsynth.git

```

In the code below, we load the `dataprep` function alongside `numpy` and `pandas`, as well as the Prop 99 dataset.

```{python}

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from mlsynth.utils.datautils import dataprep

base_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jgreathouse9/mlsynth/refs/heads/main/"
file_path = "basedata/smoking_data.csv"
url = base_url + file_path
data = pd.read_csv(url)
prepped_data = dataprep(data,data.columns[0],
data.columns[1],
data.columns[2],
data.columns[-1])
```

We then implement the DID estimator as described above, using `numpy` in this case.

```{python}

donor_mean = prepped_data['donor_matrix'].mean(axis=1)
T0 = prepped_data['pre_periods']
beta = np.mean(prepped_data['y'][:T0] - donor_mean[:T0])
yhat = donor_mean + beta

# --- Compute ATT ---
ATT = np.mean(prepped_data['y'][T0:] - yhat[T0:])
```


The pre-treatment mean difference $\approx -14.359$. So, this is what we subtract the donor mean vector entries by. When we do this, we get @fig-did-cali.


```{python}
#| label: fig-did-cali
#| fig-cap: "Difference-in-Differences, Proposition 99"
#| echo: False

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# --- Extract key info ---
T0 = prepped_data['pre_periods']
T = len(prepped_data['y'])
time = np.arange(1970, 1970 + T)

# --- Plot ---
plt.plot(time, prepped_data['y'], label='California', linewidth=2, color='black')
plt.plot(time, yhat, '--', label='DID California', linewidth=2, color='blue')

# Mark the treatment period
plt.axvline(time[T0 - 1], color='black', linestyle='--', label='Proposition 99')

plt.text(1970 + T0 + 1, min(prepped_data['y']),
         f"ATT = {ATT:.2f}",
         fontsize=12, color='red')


# --- Style ---
plt.xlabel('Time')
plt.ylabel('Cigarette Sales Per Capita')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
```
We can see here that the scalar-adjusted control group average does not appear parallel to California. The counterfactual is dissimilar to California for the first five years of the dataset in that it undershoots California's values, and for the rest of the pre-treatment period, it has higher per capita smoking than California. Why might this be? A tempting answer is sampling noise: with only a handful of controls the donor mean $\bar{y}_{0t}$ is a volatile average. That is part of the story, but it is not the part that governs whether parallel trends holds. To see the difference, return to the [linear factor model](poframe.html#assump-lfm) for untreated outcomes and ask what the DID counterfactual actually leaves behind.

::: {#lem-did-loadings .lemma title="When DID recovers the counterfactual"}
Suppose untreated outcomes follow the linear factor model
$$
y_{jt}(0) = a_j + \mathbf{b}_j^\top \mathbf{f}_t + u_{jt}, \qquad \mathbb{E}[u_{jt}] = 0,
$$
with common factors $\mathbf{f}_t$ and unit-specific loadings $\mathbf{b}_j$. Let $\bar{\mathbf{b}}_0 = N_0^{-1} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} \mathbf{b}_j$ be the average loading of the donor pool and $\bar{\mathbf{f}} = T_0^{-1} \sum_{t \in \mathcal{T}_1} \mathbf{f}_t$ the pre-period average of the factors. Then the DID counterfactual $\hat{y}_{1t}(0) = \bar{y}_{0t} + \beta^\ast$ has expected error
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[y_{1t}(0) - \hat{y}_{1t}(0)\bigr] = (\mathbf{b}_1 - \bar{\mathbf{b}}_0)^\top (\mathbf{f}_t - \bar{\mathbf{f}}).
$$
Consequently the Parallel Trends Assumption holds — and DID is unbiased for the ATT — if and only if the treated unit's loading equals the donor pool's average loading, $\mathbf{b}_1 = \bar{\mathbf{b}}_0$ (or the factors do not move over the window).
:::

The two-way fixed effects model of the [previous section](did.html#assump-twfe) is the special case in which every unit shares the same loading, so $\mathbf{b}_1 = \bar{\mathbf{b}}_0$ holds automatically and DID is exact. The lemma says DID needs much less than that — only that the treated unit's exposure to the common factors match the donor pool's *average* exposure — but it does need that, and equal weighting is what pins the requirement to the average.

The expected error isolates the failure mode. The systematic term $(\mathbf{b}_1 - \bar{\mathbf{b}}_0)^\top (\mathbf{f}_t - \bar{\mathbf{f}})$ is a *bias*: it is nonzero exactly when the treated unit loads on the common factors differently from the average donor, and it does not shrink as the panel lengthens. No quantity of pre-treatment data repairs a donor pool whose average factor exposure differs from the treated unit's — this is the violation of parallel trends. Separately, the idiosyncratic term $u_{1t} - \bar{u}_{0t}$ that the expectation averages away is a *variance*: pooling few noisy controls is unstable, and this piece does shrink with the donor count,
$$
\text{Var}\Big(\frac{1}{N_0} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} u_{jt}\Big) = \frac{1}{N_0^2} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} \text{Var}(u_{jt}).
$$
The small-$N_0$ concern, then, is really about this second, variance term; parallel trends itself is a statement about the first.

@Li2022augmented make this bias--variance split precise for exactly this class of estimators. Writing the mean squared error as $\text{MSE}(\hat{\Delta}) = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{\Delta}) + \text{Var}(\hat{\Delta})$, they show through a factor-model study that when treated and control units share the same loadings — the homogeneous case $\mathbf{b}_1 = \bar{\mathbf{b}}_0$ — DID's bias is negligible and it is in fact the *most efficient* of the standard estimators: imposing equal weights that sum to one is not a handicap but a variance-reducing restriction, and superfluous flexibility comes at a cost. When the treated unit's loading instead falls outside, or merely differs from, the donor pool's, DID's error becomes bias-dominated and its MSE the largest among the methods they compare. The Proposition 99 picture is this second regime: California's smoking trajectory does not load on the underlying factors the way the average donor state does, so equal weighting cannot align the two, and the gap in @fig-did-cali is systematic rather than noise.


This becomes even clearer when we experiment with different control units, since changing the donor pool changes its average loading $\bar{\mathbf{b}}_0$ and hence the systematic term in @lem-did-loadings. Here, we compare California to Kentucky, NC, and both jointly, using a DID model for each state individually as well as jointly. These results are presented in @fig-did-calikent. Kentucky's cigarette consumption loads on the common factors quite differently from California—its high baseline smoking rates and tobacco-based economy give it a factor exposure far from California's—so $\mathbf{b}_1 - \bar{\mathbf{b}}_0$ is large and the counterfactual tilts systematically. North Carolina differs from California in the same structural way. Demeaning removes the level gap $a_1 - \bar{a}_0$, but it cannot remove a difference in loadings; that mismatch is precisely what survives the intercept adjustment.


Practically, these violations are evident when we compare the ATT estimates. Using all controls, the naive DID yields an ATT of roughly $-27$. Using Kentucky alone yields $-29$, NC alone yields $-34$, and the Kentucky + NC combination gives $-32.36$. That the estimate swings this much with the choice of donor is the bias term at work: each pool has a different average loading, so each implies a different systematic gap, and with few controls no single pool sits close to California's factor exposure. This is a concrete illustration of why parallel trends may fail in real-world, small-$N$ applications like Proposition 99 — and, per @Li2022augmented, why more flexible weighting schemes than DID's equal weights can be worth the added variance when the donor pool is heterogeneous.

```{python}
#| label: fig-did-calikent
#| fig-cap: "Difference-in-Differences, Different Controls"
#| echo: False

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# --- Treated unit ---
california = prepped_data["y"]
T0 = prepped_data["pre_periods"]
time = np.arange(1970, 1970 + len(california))

# --- Control units ---
kentucky = prepped_data["Ywide"]["Kentucky"].values
NC = prepped_data["Ywide"]["North Carolina"].values

# --- DID for Kentucky ---
beta_kentucky = np.mean(california[:T0] - kentucky[:T0])
yhat_kentucky = kentucky + beta_kentucky
ATT_kentucky = np.mean(california[T0:] - yhat_kentucky[T0:])

# --- DID for NC ---
beta_NC = np.mean(california[:T0] - NC[:T0])
yhat_NC = NC + beta_NC
ATT_NC = np.mean(california[T0:] - yhat_NC[T0:])

# --- DID for Kentucky + NC jointly ---
donor_avg = (kentucky + NC) / 2
beta_joint = np.mean(california[:T0] - donor_avg[:T0])
yhat_joint = donor_avg + beta_joint
ATT_joint = np.mean(california[T0:] - yhat_joint[T0:])

# --- Plot subfigures ---
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14,5), sharey=True)

# Left: Observed trajectories
axes[0].plot(time, california, label='California (Observed)', linewidth=2, color='black')
axes[0].plot(time, kentucky, label='Kentucky (Observed)', linewidth=2, color='gray')
axes[0].plot(time, NC, label='NC (Observed)', linewidth=2, color='blue')
axes[0].axvline(time[T0 - 1], color='black', linestyle='--', label='Proposition 99')
axes[0].set_title('Observed Cigarette Sales')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Year')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Cigarette Sales Per Capita')
axes[0].legend()

# Right: DID counterfactuals
axes[1].plot(time, california, label='California (Observed)', linewidth=2, color='black')
axes[1].plot(time, yhat_kentucky, '--', label='Kentucky DID Counterfactual', linewidth=2, color='blue')
axes[1].plot(time, yhat_NC, '--', label='NC DID Counterfactual', linewidth=2, color='red')
axes[1].plot(time, yhat_joint, '--', label='Kentucky+NC DID', linewidth=2, color='green')
axes[1].axvline(time[T0 - 1], color='black', linestyle='--', label='Proposition 99')
axes[1].text(1970 + T0 + 1, min(california)+140, f"ATT Kentucky = {ATT_kentucky:.2f}", color='blue')
axes[1].text(1970 + T0 + 1, min(california)+120, f"ATT NC = {ATT_NC:.2f}", color='red')
axes[1].text(1970 + T0 + 1, min(california)+100, f"ATT Joint = {ATT_joint:.2f}", color='green')
axes[1].set_title('DID Counterfactuals')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Year')
axes[1].legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

It is important to emphasize that this is not a fault of DID per se. The DID method performs as intended when its key assumptions hold: SUTVA (no interference between units) and parallel trends. In the chapters that follow, we will explore other forms of looking at this problem, which address these issues by constructing a weighted combination of controls to better match the treated unit’s pre-treatment trajectory, mitigating the impact of non-parallel trends and small $N$ challenges.



::: {.problem title="Small N and Pre-Treatment Variation" data-link="#problem-small-n"}
In settings with a small number of control units, the pre-treatment average of the controls can fluctuate due to idiosyncratic shocks in individual units.

a) Explain why this makes the pre-treatment trajectory of the donor mean potentially unstable.  

b) How does this instability affect the accuracy of the DID counterfactual for the treated unit?  

c) What role does the number of control units ($N$) play in mitigating or exacerbating this problem?
:::





::: {.problem title="Observed vs. Counterfactual Trajectories" data-link="#problem-observed-vs-counterfactual"}
Suppose California’s pre-treatment trajectory is steadily decreasing while Kentucky’s is flat. Sketch and describe what the DID counterfactual using Kentucky would look like. Why might this estimate be biased?
:::

::: {.problem title="Multiple Donors" data-link="#problem-multiple-donors"}
Conceptually, why might using multiple control units (e.g., Kentucky + NC) improve the counterfactual estimate? Under what circumstances might it still fail?
:::

::: {.problem title="Interpretation of ATT" data-link="#problem-interpretation-att"}
If a DID analysis gives an ATT of –32, what does this mean in terms of California’s cigarette consumption post–Proposition 99?
:::

::: {.problem title="Limitations of DID" data-link="#problem-limitations-did"}
Discuss at least two situations in which DID may give biased estimates, even if implemented correctly. Relate your answer to the additive factor model.
:::

::: {.problem title="Arithmetic Mean as a DID Counterfactual" data-link="#problem-arithmetic-mean-did"}
In previous chapters, we defined the arithmetic average as a weighted sum where each member’s weight is proportional to its size in the sequence. In the context of using the arithmetic mean of control units to construct a DID counterfactual:

1. Explain what assumptions are being made implicitly about the control units when we take a simple arithmetic mean. Consider both their contribution to the counterfactual and their relative similarity to the treated unit.  

2. Discuss potential limitations of this assumption. Under what circumstances might the arithmetic mean of controls misrepresent the “true” counterfactual trajectory?
:::

::: {.proof title="Demeaning" data-link="did.html#proof-demean" data-target="did.html#lem-demean-trends"}
By definition,
$$
r_{jt} = y_{jt} - \frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}_1|}\sum_{s \in \mathcal{T}_1} y_{js}
= (\mu_j+\lambda_t+\varepsilon_{jt}) - \frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}_1|}\sum_{s \in \mathcal{T}_1}(\mu_j+\lambda_s+\varepsilon_{js}).
$$
Because summation is linear,
$$
\frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}_1|}\sum_{s \in \mathcal{T}_1}(\mu_j+\lambda_s+\varepsilon_{js})
= \mu_j + \bar{\lambda} + \bar{\varepsilon}_j.
$$
Subtracting this average from $y_{jt}$ gives
$$
r_{jt} = (\mu_j+\lambda_t+\varepsilon_{jt}) - (\mu_j+\bar{\lambda}+\bar{\varepsilon}_j)
= (\lambda_t-\bar{\lambda}) + (\varepsilon_{jt}-\bar{\varepsilon}_j),
$$
which proves the claim.
:::


::: {.proof title="DID Counterfactual" data-link="#proof-ddcf"}

Expanding the squared Euclidean norm gives

$$
f(\beta) = \sum_{t=1}^{T_0} \big(y_{1t} - \bar{y}_{0t} - \beta\big)^2.
$$

For clarity, define the inner function

$$
u_t(\beta) = y_{1t} - \bar{y}_{0t} - \beta,
$$

so that

$$
f(\beta) = \sum_{t=1}^{T_0} u_t(\beta)^2.
$$

To differentiate $u_t(\beta)^2$ with respect to $\beta$, we apply [the chain rule](math.html#def-chain-rule). Here, the outer function is $g(u) = u^2$ with derivative $g'(u) = 2u$, and the inner function is $h(\beta) = u_t(\beta) = y_{1t} - \bar{y}_{0t} - \beta$ with

$$
\frac{\mathrm{d} h}{\mathrm{d} \beta} = -1,
$$

since $y_{1t}$ and $\bar{y}_{0t}$ are constants with respect to $\beta$. Applying the chain rule, we find the derivative of each term:

$$
\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \beta} u_t(\beta)^2 = 2 u_t(\beta) \cdot (-1) = -2 \big(y_{1t} - \bar{y}_{0t} - \beta\big).
$$

Summing over all $T_0$ pre-treatment periods, by linearity of the derivative, gives

$$
\frac{\mathrm{d} f}{\mathrm{d} \beta} = -2 \sum_{t=1}^{T_0} \big(y_{1t} - \bar{y}_{0t} - \beta\big).
$$

Setting this derivative equal to zero, which is our first-order condition (FOC), we have

$$
-2 \sum_{t=1}^{T_0} \big(y_{1t} - \bar{y}_{0t} - \beta\big) = 0.
$$

Dividing both sides by $-2$ yields

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{T_0} \big(y_{1t} - \bar{y}_{0t} - \beta\big) = 0.
$$

Distributing the summation and noting that $\beta$ is constant across $t$:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{T_0} (y_{1t} - \bar{y}_{0t}) - \sum_{t=1}^{T_0} \beta = \sum_{t=1}^{T_0} (y_{1t} - \bar{y}_{0t}) - T_0 \beta = 0.
$$

Now we solve for beta. To do this we add $T_0 \beta$ to the RHS. That gives us this result:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{T_0} (y_{1t} - \bar{y}_{0t}) = T_0 \beta
$$

Now to isolate beta, we divide both sides by $T_0$, noting that division by a scalar $T_0$ is the same as multiplying by the reciprocal of that scalar:

$$
\beta^\ast = \frac{1}{T_0} \sum_{t=1}^{T_0} (y_{1t} - \bar{y}_{0t}).
$$

This shows that $\beta^\ast$ is simply the average pre-treatment difference between the treated unit and the control group. Under parallel trends, this difference would hold in expectation, even absent treatment. Finally, the post-treatment counterfactual for the treated unit is simply the control group mean adjusted by this optimal $\beta^\ast$:

$$
\hat{y}_{1t}(0) = \bar{y}_{0t} + \beta^\ast, \quad \forall \: t \in \mathcal{T}_2
$$

:::




::: {.proof title="Centering Lemma" data-link="#proof-centering" data-target="#lem-centering"}

For any fixed donor pool $\mathcal{N}_0$, we know from [this proof](did.html#proof-ddcf)

$$
\beta^\ast = \frac{1}{T_0} \sum_{t=1}^{T_0} \bigl(y_{1t} - \bar{y}_{\mathcal{N}_0,t}\bigr)
\qquad\text{where}\qquad
\bar{y}_{\mathcal{N}_0,t} = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_0|} \sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}_0} y_{jt}.
$$

Here is the residual:

$$
r_t = y_{1t} 
      - \frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_0|} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} y_{jt} 
      - \beta^\ast
\qquad\text{where}\qquad
\beta^\ast = \frac{1}{T_0} \sum_{t=1}^{T_0} \left( y_{1t} 
      - \frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_0|} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} y_{jt} \right).
$$

Substitute $\beta^\ast$ directly into the residual:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_t &= y_{1t} 
       - \frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_0|} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} y_{jt} 
       - \frac{1}{T_0} \sum_{t=1}^{T_0} \left( y_{1t} 
       - \frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_0|} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} y_{jt} \right) \\[12pt]
    &= y_{1t} 
       - \frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_0|} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} y_{jt} 
       - \frac{1}{T_0} \sum_{t=1}^{T_0} y_{1t} 
       + \frac{1}{T_0} \sum_{t=1}^{T_0} \left( \frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_0|} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} y_{jt} \right).
\end{aligned}
$$

Now recognize the averages:

$$
\frac{1}{T_0} \sum_{t=1}^{T_0} y_{1t} = \bar{y}_1
$$

and

$$
\frac{1}{T_0} \sum_{t=1}^{T_0} \left( \frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_0|} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} y_{jt} \right) 
  = \frac{1}{T_0} \sum_{t=1}^{T_0} \bar{y}_{\mathcal{N}_0,t} 
  = \bar{m}_{\mathcal{N}_0}.
$$

So

$$
r_t = y_{1t} - \bar{y}_1 - \frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_0|} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} y_{jt} + \bar{m}_{\mathcal{N}_0}
$$

becomes

$$
\left(y_{1t} - \bar{y}_1\right)
-
\left( \frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_0|} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} y_{jt} 
       - \bar{m}_{\mathcal{N}_0}\right),
$$

which simplifies to

$$
\left(y_{1t} - \bar{y}_1\right)
-
(\bar{y}_{\mathcal{N}_0,t} - \bar{m}_{\mathcal{N}_0}).
$$

In vector form, this is:

$$
\boxed{
\mathbf{r}
= \mathbf{y}_c - \bar{\mathbf{y}}_{c,\mathcal{N}_0}
\qquad\Rightarrow\qquad
\ell_{\text{DID}}(\mathcal{N}_0) 
= \|\mathbf{y}_c - \bar{\mathbf{y}}_{c,\mathcal{N}_0}\|_2^2
}
$$

where

$$
\mathbf{y}_c = \mathbf{y}_1 - \bar{y}_1 \, \mathbf{1}_{T_0},
\qquad
\bar{\mathbf{y}}_{c,\mathcal{N}_0} = \bar{\mathbf{y}}_{\mathcal{N}_0} - \bar{m}_{\mathcal{N}_0} \, \mathbf{1}_{T_0}.
$$

:::


::: {.proof title="When DID recovers the counterfactual" data-link="did.html#proof-did-loadings" data-target="did.html#lem-did-loadings"}

Write donor-pool averages as $\bar{a}_0 = N_0^{-1} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} a_j$ and $\bar{u}_{0t} = N_0^{-1} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_0} u_{jt}$. Averaging the factor model across the donor pool gives
$$
\bar{y}_{0t} = \bar{a}_0 + \bar{\mathbf{b}}_0^\top \mathbf{f}_t + \bar{u}_{0t},
$$
so the gap between the treated unit and the donor mean is
$$
y_{1t}(0) - \bar{y}_{0t} = (a_1 - \bar{a}_0) + (\mathbf{b}_1 - \bar{\mathbf{b}}_0)^\top \mathbf{f}_t + (u_{1t} - \bar{u}_{0t}).
$$
The DID intercept is the pre-period average of this gap over $\mathcal{T}_1$,
$$
\beta^\ast = (a_1 - \bar{a}_0) + (\mathbf{b}_1 - \bar{\mathbf{b}}_0)^\top \bar{\mathbf{f}} + (\bar{u}_1 - \bar{\bar{u}}_0),
$$
with $\bar{u}_1 = T_0^{-1} \sum_{t \in \mathcal{T}_1} u_{1t}$ and $\bar{\bar{u}}_0 = T_0^{-1} \sum_{t \in \mathcal{T}_1} \bar{u}_{0t}$. Subtracting the counterfactual $\hat{y}_{1t}(0) = \bar{y}_{0t} + \beta^\ast$ from $y_{1t}(0)$, the intercept difference $a_1 - \bar{a}_0$ cancels and
$$
y_{1t}(0) - \hat{y}_{1t}(0) = (\mathbf{b}_1 - \bar{\mathbf{b}}_0)^\top (\mathbf{f}_t - \bar{\mathbf{f}}) + (u_{1t} - \bar{u}_{0t}) - (\bar{u}_1 - \bar{\bar{u}}_0).
$$
Taking expectations and using $\mathbb{E}[u_{jt}] = 0$ leaves the systematic term
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[y_{1t}(0) - \hat{y}_{1t}(0)\bigr] = (\mathbf{b}_1 - \bar{\mathbf{b}}_0)^\top (\mathbf{f}_t - \bar{\mathbf{f}}),
$$
which vanishes for every $t$ if and only if $\mathbf{b}_1 = \bar{\mathbf{b}}_0$ or $\mathbf{f}_t = \bar{\mathbf{f}}$ for all $t$.
:::
